1: The divine council reconvenes with identical staging to Job 1: the bene ha-’elohim assemble, the adversary comes among them. The formal repetition is a literary device that frames the second test as a structural parallel to the first — same setting, same participants, same challenge-and-permission format. Ancient Near Eastern divine council scenes in literature often use this kind of formal repetition to signal a parallel narrative sequence (e.g., the two Baal cycle tablets from Ugarit follow similar patterns in their two main combat episodes).
2: The adversary’s identical response to YHWH’s question (“from roaming through the earth and walking back and forth on it”) creates a deliberate verbatim repetition. Ancient Hebrew narrative uses exact repetition of dialogue as a formal literary device (cf. the messenger speeches in Genesis that repeat commands verbatim when delivered). The repetition here emphasizes the adversary’s unchanged status and activity between the two scenes — he is still the earth-roaming patrol agent, still in the same relationship to the divine court.
3: YHWH’s acknowledgment that the adversary “incited me against him to ruin him without cause” is a remarkable theological concession. In ancient Near Eastern divine court literature, a deity acknowledging agency in a morally complex action was unusual — divine actions were typically presented as simply sovereign and therefore justified. The book of Job pushes this boundary: YHWH explicitly acknowledges that the test was the adversary’s instigation and that the destruction was causeless (hinam). This is a distinctive OT theological move that places divine sovereignty and divine goodness in genuine tension.
4: The “skin for skin” formula is a commercial or proverbial saying whose exact origins are unknown. In ancient trade contexts, the exchange rate of one commodity for another was expressed in such formulaic terms. Some interpreters read it as a reference to hide-trading: leather merchants bartering one type of skin for another, always keeping the calculation self-interested. The underlying anthropology is the adversary’s consistent claim: humans are rational self-interested maximizers, and any apparent piety is a calculated exchange for divine protection or blessing.
5: The escalation from possessions to body reflects a graduated understanding of suffering in ancient thought: losing property was serious but survivable; bodily suffering attacks the person directly and cannot be escaped even in sleep or distraction. In Mesopotamian medicine and theology, skin diseases were understood as divine punishments (the Akkadian medical text Sakikku lists diseases and their divine causes). A severe, painful, visible skin disease in the ancient world also carried the social consequence of exclusion from community — as the following verses show, Job ends up outside the city on the ash-heap.
6: YHWH’s second permission sets a new limit: the adversary may attack Job’s body but must preserve his life (nefesh). The preservation of life as the inviolable boundary within the suffering permitted to the adversary is theologically significant: whatever suffering is permitted, it stops short of death. This corresponds to the general OT understanding that YHWH holds the keys of death and life (Deuteronomy 32:39: “I kill and I make alive”) and that this ultimate power is not delegated even to the adversary.
7: The disease shehin ra’ (severe inflammatory sores) has been identified by various commentators with conditions ranging from elephantiasis to smallpox to severe eczema. The Ugaritic cognate shhn appears in a list of diseases associated with Mot (death) in Ugaritic ritual texts. In ancient medicine, skin diseases of this type were treated with scraping (as v8 describes) to remove crusts and exudate. The disease’s coverage “from the sole of his foot to the top of his head” is the merism of totality — expressing universal bodily affliction.
8: The ash-heap (’efer) where Job sits was likely a public waste disposal site outside the settlement. In ancient Israelite and Canaanite towns, the ash-heap or refuse dump was located outside the city gates and served as the social location of those excluded from community life (cf. the poor sitting on the ash-heap in 1 Samuel 2:8; Psalms 113:7). Job’s relocation to the ash-heap from his position as the city’s most respected leader (Job 29:7) represents a total social reversal: from the city gate (the place of honor and judgment) to the ash-heap (the place of refuse and outcasts).
9: Job’s wife’s counsel — “curse God and die” — has been interpreted both harshly (as Satanic temptation, as Augustine argued) and sympathetically (as the desperate suggestion of a woman who has also lost everything). In the ancient world, the wife of a paterfamilias had no independent legal standing; her social status was entirely dependent on her husband’s position. She has lost her children, her household status, and now watches her husband suffer with no relief in sight. The LXX expands her speech considerably (adding a long lament), suggesting that later tradition recognized the pathos of her position and sought to give it fuller expression.
10: Job’s rebuke — speaking “as one of the foolish women” — distinguishes between the wisdom tradition’s sage (hakham) and the fool (naval). Nabal in 1 Samuel 25 is the paradigm case: a man whose name means “fool” and who acts accordingly, disregarding social obligation and divine order. The fool’s speech is characterized in wisdom literature as acting as though there is no moral order in the universe (Psalms 14:1: “the fool says in his heart there is no God” — not intellectual atheism but practical disregard for divine moral order). Job’s response establishes that his wife’s counsel falls into this category.
11: The three friends come from specific geographical and wisdom traditions. Eliphaz the Temanite: Teman was a region of Edom associated with legendary wisdom (Jeremiah 49:7: “Is wisdom no more in Teman? Has counsel perished from the prudent? Has their wisdom vanished?”). Teman is also associated with the divine warrior march in Habakkuk 3:3. Bildad the Shuhite: Shuah appears in Genesis 25:2 as a son of Abraham and Keturah, placing him in the trans-Jordan / north Arabian region. Zophar the Naamathite: Naamah is uncertain; several locations in southern Canaan or Arabia have been proposed. All three represent the wisdom traditions of the non-Israelite peoples surrounding Israel.
12: The friends’ inability to recognize Job at a distance reflects the severity of his transformation. In the ancient world, recognition by sight was the primary social identification mechanism — a person’s appearance communicated their status, health, and identity. Job’s skin disease, ash-covered condition, and gaunt frame would make him unrecognizable as the person they knew. The three mourning gestures — weeping aloud, tearing garments, throwing dust skyward — are all attested mourning customs. The dust thrown toward heaven is an unusual gesture; it may express an appeal or protest directed toward God, expressing the mourners’ bewilderment at what they see.
13: The seven-day silent mourning is the standard full mourning period in the ancient world. In Mesopotamian practice, seven-day mourning for the dead was common (cf. the Gilgamesh epic: Gilgamesh mourns Enkidu for seven days). In the OT, seven-day mourning appears for Jacob (Genesis 50:10), Saul (1 Samuel 31:13), and as the Ezekiel mourning requirement (Ezekiel 3:15: Ezekiel sat among the exiles for seven days, overwhelmed). The silence of the friends during this period is remarkable: they observe Job’s suffering, assess its severity as overwhelming, and choose presence-without-words over the premature comfort that would characterize all their subsequent speeches.
